description: Figure 2 presents room-temperature compressive stress-strain curves for [112]-oriented Fe-16%Al monocrystals tested at a compression rate of 10^-4 s^-1, comparing four prior heat-treatment conditions: as-grown, 1100 °C furnace cooling (FC), 1100 °C FC down to 600 °C with subsequent water quench (600 °C Q), and water quench from 1100 °C followed by annealing at 300 °C for 14 days (1100 °C Q + 300 °C/14d). The curves for the as-grown, FC, and FC+600 °C Q specimens cluster closely, showing similar yield stresses around 420–450 MPa and comparable work-hardening behavior up to 5–6% strain. In contrast, the 1100 °C Q + 300 °C/14d curve displays a distinctly higher yield stress (~460 MPa) and stronger work hardening, attaining a flow stress near 500 MPa at larger strains. This comparison indicates that interrupting furnace cooling at 600 °C does not markedly alter the mechanical response, whereas quenching from 1100 °C—which introduces excess vacancies—significantly increases strength. The figure supports the core conclusion that only heat treatments at temperatures above 600 °C substantially affect the deformation behavior of Fe-Al alloys.
